𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Five -- Complete

The Rule of the Congregation

SEREKH HA-EDAH -- 1QSa

Appendix to the Community Rule -- The Eschatological Assembly

Columns 1 and 2

This Is the Rule for All the Congregation of Yisra'EL in the Last Days -- When Creator Begets the Messiah Among Them -- The Priest Shall Come First -- The Messianic Banquet -- This Is the Session of the Men of Renown

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 1 Qumran -- 1QSa (1Q28a) -- Hebrew Primary

Appendix to the Community Rule -- Same Cave 1 Scroll

The Eschatological Assembly at the End of Days

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

RULE OF THE CONGREGATION -- 1QSa -- COLUMNS 1 AND 2

RULE OF THE CONGREGATION — COLUMN 1 This Is the Rule for All the Congregation of Yisra'EL — In the Last Days — They Shall Walk in the Order of the Sons of Tzadok — From Youth They Shall Be Instructed — Their Stages of Life in the Community

1 This is the rule for all the congregation of Yisra'EL in the last days, when they assemble in community to walk according to the order of the sons of Tzadok the priests and the men of their covenant who turned away from walking in the way of the people.

2 These are the men of his counsel who have kept his covenant in the midst of wickedness to atone for the land.

3 When they come they shall assemble all those who come, including women and children, and they shall read into their ears all the statutes of the covenant, and shall instruct them in all its ordinances, that they may not stray in their errors.

4 And this is the rule for all the hosts of the congregation: everyone who is born in Yisra'EL shall be instructed in the book of HAGY from his youth and shall be educated in the covenant precepts according to his age.

5 For ten years he shall be counted among the young children. At the age of twenty years he shall be enrolled to pass among those counted, and he shall join his family and be admitted to the holy congregation.

6 He shall not approach a woman to know her by lying with her before he is fully twenty years of age, when he knows good and evil.

7 And after that she shall be received to bear witness against him for the statutes of the Torah.

8 At the age of twenty-five years he may take his stand in the service of the holy congregation, to work in the service of the congregation.

9 At the age of thirty years he may approach to stand in judgment and to take part in disputes of the law and to stand with the heads of thousands of Yisra'EL.

10 These are the leaders of the congregation, those called to the assembly, the men of renown of the congregation, the heads of the tribes of Yisra'EL, and their judges.

11 All the heads of the fathers of the congregation together with the wise men of the holy congregation shall take their seats before him, each according to his dignity.

12 And no man smitten with any human uncleanness shall enter the assembly of Creator. And no man smitten in his flesh, paralyzed in his feet or hands, lame or blind or deaf or dumb shall enter the congregation.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Rule of the Congregation (1QSa) was found in Cave 1 at Qumran in 1947 as part of the same scroll collection that contained the Community Rule (1QS). It is officially designated 1Q28a and is understood as an appendix or companion document to the Community Rule. Where the Community Rule governs the present community during the age of wickedness, 1QSa governs the eschatological congregation of Yisra'EL at the end of days — the assembled covenant people when the Messiahs of Aharon and Yisra'EL are present. Column 1 establishes the age-based structure of the covenant congregation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve the Rule of the Congregation. The age-based structure of Column 1 — twenty for enrollment, twenty-five for service, thirty for judgment — reflects the Levitical service ages of Bamidbar 4:3 and Bamidbar 8:24, which the Orit Ge'ez preserves in full. The Qumran community applies the same Levitical age structure to its eschatological congregation: the covenant people at the end of days will be organised by the same divinely ordained age framework as the original Levitical service.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 4:3 — from thirty years old to fifty years old, all that enter into the service, to do the work in the tabernacle of meeting — and Bamidbar 8:24 — from twenty-five years old upward they shall go in to perform service — provide the foundational covenant precedents for Column 1's age stages. The Rule of the Congregation preserves and extends these Mosaic covenant regulations into the eschatological community framework.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — no man smitten with any human uncleanness shall enter the assembly of Creator. The exclusion of those with physical disabilities from the eschatological assembly has been a subject of scholarly discussion. The Rule of the Congregation's exclusion is based on the Levitical purity requirements for priestly service — Vayikra 21:17-23 — which the Qumran community applies to the entire congregation at the end of days because the eschatological assembly will be conducted in the presence of the holy angels. The exclusion is not a permanent judgment on the persons involved but a ritual purity requirement for the specific context of the divine assembly.

RULE OF THE CONGREGATION — COLUMN 2 The Session of the Men of Renown — When the Messiah Is Among Them — The Priest Shall Come First — Then the Messiah of Yisra'EL — This Is the Session of the Men of Renown at the End of Days

1 This is the session of the men of renown called to the appointed place of meeting when Creator begets the Messiah among them.

2 The priest shall come at the head of the whole congregation of Yisra'EL, and all the fathers of the sons of Aharon the priests.

3 And they shall sit before him, each according to his dignity.

4 And afterward the Messiah of Yisra'EL shall enter, and the heads of the thousands of Yisra'EL shall sit before him.

5 Each according to his dignity, according to his standing in their camps and according to their marches.

6 And all the heads of the fathers of the congregation together with the wise men of the holy congregation shall sit before them, each according to his dignity.

7 When they gather together at the table of the community to drink wine, and the table of the community is prepared and the wine is mixed for drinking,

8 No man shall stretch out his hand to the first portion of the bread or the wine before the priest.

9 For he shall bless the first portion of the bread and the wine and shall stretch out his hand over the bread first.

10 And afterward the Messiah of Yisra'EL shall stretch out his hands over the bread.

11 And afterward all the congregation of the community shall bless, each according to his dignity.

12 They shall proceed according to this statute at every meal where ten men are gathered together.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QSa Column 2 contains the most detailed description of the messianic banquet in all the Qumran literature. The seating order — priest first, Messiah of Yisra'EL second, heads of thousands third — confirms the Community Rule's dual-Messiah expectation and establishes the precise liturgical protocol for the eschatological meal. The Messiah of Yisra'EL is explicitly subordinate to the priest at the meal — the priestly Messiah of Aharon takes precedence. This protocol is practised proleptically in the community's daily meals, which follow the same order.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve the Rule of the Congregation. The covenant meal theology of Column 2 — the blessing of bread and wine in a communal assembly with a hierarchical seating order — resonates with the covenant meal traditions of the Orit Ge'ez liturgical theology. The blessing of bread before eating — with the priest blessing first — reflects the covenant conviction that food is received from Creator and every meal is an act of covenant acknowledgment.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition preserves no parallel to the messianic banquet of 1QSa Column 2. The priestly blessing of food before eating is rooted in the Masoretic Devarim 8:10 — when you have eaten and are satisfied you shall bless YHWH your Creator — but the Qumran eschatological meal protocol is a developed covenant institutional expression of this foundational obligation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — when Creator begets the Messiah among them. The Aramaic phrase — yoled El et ha-mashiach — is one of the most disputed phrases in the Qumran corpus. The word yoled can mean begets, causes to arise, or brings forth. The question of whether the Messiah is here described as divinely begotten — which would imply a unique divine-human relationship — or simply divinely appointed to arise has no consensus resolution. The majority scholarly reading is that the phrase means when Creator causes the Messiah to arise in their midst — a functional appointment rather than an ontological claim. The covenant community's dual-Messiah theology is based on appointment and function, not on divine nature.

RULE OF THE CONGREGATION -- COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Five -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THIS IS THE SESSION OF THE MEN OF RENOWN

WHEN CREATOR BEGETS THE MESSIAH AMONG THEM

Rule of the Congregation 1QSa Column 2 -- Dead Sea Scrolls
